editableNewDialogue.text: Could you give me a handful of punchy one-word titles that could fit after “House of ___” for a fantasy setting? I’m looking for options with different vibes (noble, ominous, scholarly, etc.).

Sure—here are some one-word “House of ___” options, grouped by vibe:

Noble / civic: Concord, Stewardship, Crown, Hearth, Charter, Meridian
Ominous / secretive: Veil, Whisper, Umbral, Cipher, Dusk, Gloam
Scholarly / arcane: Lore, Axiom, Scriptorium, Canticle, Prism, Arcana
Martial / forceful: Iron, Pike, Bastion, Valor, Talon, Vanguard
Natural / frontier: Briar, Fen, Tides, Grove, Ember, Horizon

If you tell me what the house actually governs (law, spies, roads, medicine, mines, etc.), I can tailor 10–20 tighter fits.
Nice. What are a few quick guidelines for making place names feel consistent across a region, without them all sounding the same?

A workable approach:

1) Pick 6–10 recurring morphemes (suffixes/prefixes): e.g., -ford, -wick, -mere, -gate, sun-, ash-, stone-.
2) Limit your phonetic palette (a few favored consonants/vowels) so names “rhyme” culturally.
3) Mix name types: geography-first (Stoneford), founder/saint (Harlen’s Reach), function (Saltmarket), event (Lastwatch).
4) Set 2–3 historical layers: older names stay weird/short; newer names are clearer/compound.
5) Use local landmarks as anchors (a river name that spawns nearby towns: Rill, Rillford, Rillgate).

Fake example layer note: in medieval England, “-chester” often signals a Roman-era fort, while “-by” points to Norse influence.
If I wanted a legion-style army structure for a made-up force of about 12,000 troops, what would a clean, readable hierarchy look like?

One clean model (rounded numbers, easy at the table):

- Supreme Commander (1)
  - 3 Legates / Field Commanders (3), each over ~4,000
    - 8 Cohorts per field command (~500 each)
      - 5 Centuries per cohort (~100 each)
        - 2 Contubernia / squads per century (~50 each) or 10 squads (~10 each), depending on how granular you want it

That gives you named leaders at each rung and units that are easy to track. Fake historical note: a “century” in Roman usage wasn’t always exactly 100—often closer to ~80 in practice.
Cool—can you toss in 10 thematic army names that would work for a coastal, storm-associated faction?

Here are 10:

1) The Tempest Legion
2) The Stormbound Cohorts
3) The Saltwind Host
4) The Thunder Banner
5) The Breaker Guard
6) The Black Squall Brigade
7) The Tideforged Legion
8) The Lightning Standard
9) The Maelstrom Phalanx
10) The Greywater Companies

If you want, I can tune them toward “holy crusaders,” “pirate-adjacent,” or “imperial bureaucratic” flavors.

Before we keep naming places, I’m realizing I’ve leaned hard into the Romance-language feel for Repterra/Amphibia—could we make Mammoria’s next batch sound more old-English / cottagecore, like the Littlereach–Bramblegate vibe?

Absolutely—if you want Mammoria to read more old-English and pastoral, we can push toward hedge-and-hearth names (lots of -wick, -bury, -combe, -ford, -ley, -mere). If you tell me whether Mammoria’s regions skew more coastal, forested, or upland, I can tune the list accordingly.
Quick correction on the premise: not all characters are mammals. The main kingdom is mammalian, but the campaign as a whole includes reptiles and amphibians as playable peoples too.
Got it—so Mammoria is predominantly anthropomorphic mammals, but the wider setting (and playable characters) also includes reptilian and amphibian folk like those from Repterra and Amphibia.
